pmos

Product-Market Fit (PMF) — Primer

Created using pmos‑learnkit

Primer · PM Tactics — Strategy. Product-market fit is the most invoked and least pinned-down idea in product work. This primer maps the canon rather than arguing a single line. The spine: Marc Andreessen's 2007 framing — "being in a good market with a product that can satisfy that market," and "the only thing that matters" — sits on Andy Rachleff's older law that market beats team and product. Andreessen describes fit as something you feel; the field has spent the years since trying to make that feeling measurable. Two instruments dominate. Sean Ellis's 40% survey ("how would you feel if you could no longer use this product?") is the leading indicator, operationalized into a repeatable engine by Rahul Vohra at Superhuman. Cohort retention curves that flatten instead of decaying to zero are the behavioral proof. The modern consensus — Rob Walling, Bessemer, CRV, GoPractice — is that fit is a spectrum, not a switch, and one you can lose. The counter-melody comes from Brian Balfour, who argues PMF is necessary but not sufficient: you also need product-channel, channel-model, and model-market fit. Where the camps genuinely disagree — whether fit is a number or a feeling, whether 40% is a law or a heuristic, whether PMF is "the only thing that matters" — this primer flags it rather than papering over it. 17 verified sources.

What product-market fit actually is

The cleanest definition is still the oldest. Andreessen calls product/market fit "being in a good market with a product that can satisfy that market." Wikipedia's synthesis sharpens it to "the degree to which a product satisfies a strong market demand" — and the word degree is doing quiet work there, foreshadowing the spectrum debate below. CRV's 2026 restatement keeps the same shape with a sharper edge: PMF is "the point where a real market wants what you're building and your product reliably satisfies that demand." The recurring nouns are market, demand, and satisfaction — never the product in isolation.

The term has a contested lineage worth knowing, because it explains why so many definitions coexist. Per the historical record, the underlying thinking traces to Don Valentine at Sequoia, the phrase was first named by Andy Rachleff of Benchmark, and Andreessen popularized it in the mid-2000s. No single author owns it, which is partly why the concept resists a single operational definition — each camp inherited the words and supplied its own instruments.

What everyone agrees on is the negative space. PMF is not a feature set, not a launch, not a funding round, and not a moment of founder conviction. It is a property of the relationship between a product and a market, observed from the market's side. That framing — fit as something the market does to you, not something you declare — is the thread that runs through every section that follows.

"The only thing that matters"

Andreessen's essay earns its status less from the definition than from the priority claim. He argues that for an early-stage startup, "the only thing that matters is getting to product/market fit" — and that before you have it, almost nothing else you optimize will save you, while after you have it, many sins are forgiven. This is deliberately absolutist, and it was meant as a corrective to founders polishing teams, decks, and features while the core question went unanswered.

The claim rests on a borrowed law. Andreessen quotes Rachleff: "When a great team meets a lousy market, market wins. When a lousy team meets a great market, market wins." The market is the dominant variable; the job is to find one that is already pulling. Paul Graham makes the same point from the demand side in How to Get Startup Ideas: "When a startup launches, there have to be at least some users who really need what they're making — not just people who could see themselves using it one day, but who want it urgently." Urgency, not breadth, is the early test.

This is the most quotable position in the canon, and also the one most directly disputed — Balfour's "isn't enough" argument, covered later, is a direct rebuttal to the word only. Hold the tension: "the only thing that matters" is a brilliant forcing function for a pre-fit team and an actively misleading one for a post-fit company that still has to solve distribution and economics. Which camp is right depends almost entirely on what stage you are in.

The market comes first

If market is the dominant variable, the practical implication is an ordering rule: find the market before you fall in love with the product. Brian Balfour makes this the literal name of the thing, reframing "Product Market Fit" as "Market Product Fit" — "Start with the market (problem), then the product (solution). Not the other way around." The failure mode he names is universal and seductive: "a solution looking for a problem."

Balfour's market-definition discipline is more specific than the usual "talk to customers." He insists on defining four things up front — Category, Who, Problems, and Motivations — and then makes a pointed claim about where teams go shallow: "defining the problems and the motivations behind those problems is far more important" than nailing the category or the audience. Most teams can describe who their user is; far fewer can articulate the motivation underneath the stated problem, which is exactly where differentiated products are found.

There is a subtlety the market-first camp is careful about: "market first" does not mean "build nothing and survey." It means the product is a probe into the market, and the market's response — not the product's elegance — is the signal you are reading. Andreessen's felt symptoms of fit, in the next section, are all market-side reactions. The product is the question; PMF is the market's answer.

PMF is a spectrum, not a switch

The single biggest correction the field has made to Andreessen's framing is to kill the binary. Rob Walling names the bad mental model directly: "They treat it like a light switch. Either you have it or you don't." His replacement: "Product-market fit is a spectrum. You move through stages, and each stage requires different focus areas." He lays out five — Pre-PMF, Weak, Emerging, Strong, and Mature — and reframes the operative question from "do I have PMF?" to "what stage am I in, and what should I focus on to strengthen it?"

This is now close to consensus, expressed in nearly identical language across the venture canon. Bessemer: "PMF isn't binary; it's a spectrum," from light to moderate to strong signal. CRV tiers it Early, Developing, Strong, Extreme. GoPractice frames strength operationally as share of choice: "If a user segment uses a product in 100% of situations, then the product has reached the maximum product/market fit strength" — at 10%, alternatives won the other 90% of the time. Even Wikipedia now leads with "not binary—it exists on a spectrum."

One genuine disagreement survives inside the consensus, and it is worth flagging rather than smoothing: whether the spectrum is a measurable quantity or a directional feeling. GoPractice and the retention camp treat strength as a number (share of choice, retention plateau height). Andreessen's original framing — and the qualitative camp in the next section — treat it as something you recognize, not something you compute. Most practitioners now hold both: use the number to argue with, but do not mistake a 41% survey score for a finished job.

What it feels like (the qualitative read)

Before the instruments, the canon trusts the senses — and the felt descriptions are unusually consistent. Andreessen's is the most cited: "The customers are buying the product just as fast as you can make it—or usage is growing just as fast as you can add more servers." The absence is just as recognizable: "the customers aren't quite getting value out of the product, word of mouth isn't spreading, usage isn't growing that fast." Fit, in this telling, is a change in who is doing the pushing.

Lenny Rachitsky's curation surfaces the sharpest metaphor for that shift, from Emmett Shear: "Pushing a boulder: don't have product/market fit. Chasing a boulder: have product/market fit. Both are very demanding, but feel totally different." The work does not get easier after fit; the direction of the force reverses. Pre-fit, every bit of growth is something you manufacture. Post-fit, demand runs ahead of you and the job becomes keeping up.

The qualitative read has an obvious weakness, which the survey camp exists to fix: feelings lag and flatter. Founders are primed to perceive fit they do not have, and the loudest early users are unrepresentative. The learningloop guide states the trap as a koan — "If you have to ask whether you have Product/Market Fit, the answer is simple: you don't" — which is useful as a gut check and dangerous as a methodology, because plenty of teams with real, early, narrow fit are right to be asking. The felt signals are necessary, not sufficient; the next two sections are how the field made them auditable.

The Sean Ellis test (the leading indicator)

The most widely adopted instrument is Sean Ellis's survey question, posed to active users: "How would you feel if you could no longer use [this product]?" with four options — Very disappointed, Somewhat disappointed, Not disappointed, and N/A (no longer use). The headline rule is the 40% benchmark: when 40% or more answer "very disappointed," you very likely have fit. The Sean Ellis Score is just that proportion — (very disappointed ÷ respondents) × 100 — from the same Ellis who coined "growth hacking."

Its power is that it is a leading indicator. Where retention and revenue tell you about fit you already had, the survey detects the must-have intensity that predicts fit before the growth curve confirms it. That is precisely why it became the cornerstone of the Superhuman engine in the next section. It also segments cleanly: the question sorts users into those who would grieve the product and those who would shrug, which is far more actionable than an average satisfaction score.

The 40% figure deserves an explicit caveat, because it is routinely cited as a law and was never one. Ellis derived it empirically — products that scaled tended to clear it — but it is a heuristic, not a constant, and varies by category, sample, and the maturity of the user being surveyed. The learningloop glossary is careful here: the test "is just one part of understanding your users" and "should be used in conjunction with other strategies such as customer feedback, market research, and iterative product development." Treat 40% as a goalpost that focuses a team, not a finish line that certifies one. Sample matters too: surveying your most engaged cohort will flatter the score; surveying recent signups will deflate it.

The Superhuman engine (operationalizing the test)

Rahul Vohra's contribution was not a new metric but a system for moving one. Facing the usual fog — Superhuman's initial "very disappointed" score was a middling 22% — he built a four-step engine around Ellis's question, documented in First Round Review. It is the most concrete PMF playbook in the canon because it turns a diagnostic into a roadmap.

Step one: segment to your supporters. Isolate the "very disappointed" users and build a detailed profile of them — Vohra's "High-Expectation Customer" — to narrow the target market and align the team on who you are actually for. Step two: convert the on-the-fence. Ignore the "not disappointed" (they are lost causes) and mine the "somewhat disappointed" segment whose main benefit already matches your core strength — they are the reachable upside. Step three: split the roadmap, roughly half to doubling down on what supporters love and half to removing the blockers the convertibles named. Step four: repeat relentlessly, surveying continuously and making the "very disappointed" percentage the team's primary metric, rebuilt quarterly.

The result is the canon's most cited proof point: Superhuman's score climbed from 22% to 58% in three quarters. The deeper lesson is methodological. Vohra treats PMF not as a state to be discovered but as a number to be deliberately engineered upward — the opposite of the "you'll just know" school. The engine's most underrated move is step two's filter: you do not chase every dissatisfied user, only the ones whose desired benefit is already your strength, which keeps the roadmap from being captured by people you will never satisfy.

Retention curves (the quantitative proof)

If the survey is the leading indicator, cohort retention is the lagging proof — and most of the canon treats it as the single most honest one. The test is shape, not level: plot the percentage of a cohort still active over time and ask whether the curve flattens. Lenny Rachitsky, curating Brian Balfour, states it plainly: "If it flattens off at some point, you have probably found product/market fit for some market or audience." A curve that decays to zero means everyone eventually leaves; a curve that flattens means a durable set of users found lasting value.

But the plateau's height matters as much as its existence. Lenny's retention piece warns that a curve which flattens near 10% is still bad — "only 10% of your users stick around long-term... generally a bad retention rate" — because a low plateau means fit for a sliver too thin to build on. The strongest products show a "smile": retention that flattens and then ticks upward as resurrected and expanding users outweigh churn. Dan Olsen, quoted in OPEXEngine's roundup, makes the case for why behavior beats opinion here: "Retention rate is the best measure of product-market fit, because it is a behavioral measure of repeat usage." People vote with their feet, not their survey answers.

The catch worth naming: retention is honest but slow. By the time a twelve-month curve has flattened convincingly, the bets that produced it are a year old. This is the structural reason the field pairs a leading instrument (the survey) with a lagging one (the curve) — and why a16z's "product-user fit" argument, below, exists to give very early teams something to read before either instrument has enough data.

Leading versus lagging signals

Reading PMF well means knowing which clock each signal runs on. Leading signals move first and predict: the Sean Ellis "very disappointed" score, qualitative pull, growing organic word-of-mouth, and early engagement intensity. Lagging signals confirm after the fact: cohort retention plateaus, net revenue retention, churn, and durable revenue. A team that reads only lagging signals learns the truth too late to act on it; a team that reads only leading signals risks believing a survey over behavior.

OPEXEngine's roundup is a useful catalog of the lagging, quantitative end, with the caveat that the thresholds are category-specific and cited from named investors rather than universal: Andrew Chen's "less than 2% monthly churn," a path to $100k MRR, and 5% free-to-paid conversion; net dollar retention above 100% with logo retention above 80% for the expansion stage. CRV adds a texture distinction that cuts across all of these: "Organic growth is harder to fake" than paid, and "Retention is the most honest signal of product-market fit," while "persistent high churn despite product improvements" is the clearest red flag.

Bessemer's playbook adds signals tuned for products where the old metrics lag too slowly — relevant well beyond AI. They recommend retention measured "by use case," Time to Value (because "if it takes too long to show the magic, churn will follow"), and a "second-bite usage rate" — whether users come back to repeat the behavior after their first success. They also insist on separating durable revenue (recurring, contractual) from experimental revenue (pilots, novelty spikes), because a revenue line inflated by curiosity is a lagging signal that lies. The practical synthesis across the camps: triangulate. No single number is fit; a leading signal you can act on plus a lagging signal you can trust is the working definition.

Start with a beachhead, not the whole market

Nearly every camp converges on the same tactical advice: do not chase the broad market first. Win a narrow, intense segment completely, then expand. a16z formalizes the pre-stage as "product-user fit" — "the extent to which you've built the right product for the right user" — and punctures a common self-deception: "The honest version of 'we've got product-market fit on a small cohort of very early power users' is 'we've got product-user fit.'" Product-user fit, they argue, "is almost always the thing that matters right before the only thing that ultimately matters."

This dovetails with Graham's urgency test — "there have to be at least some users who really need what they're making... who want it urgently" — and with Vohra's High-Expectation Customer. All three are saying the same thing from different angles: depth of need in a narrow group beats shallow appeal across a wide one. A small group that would grieve the product's loss is a foundation; a large group that mildly likes it is a trap, because the mild signal is too weak to build retention or word-of-mouth on.

The beachhead logic also resolves the "do I have PMF?" anxiety productively. The answer is usually "yes, for someone, and not yet for everyone" — which is exactly the spectrum framing made tactical. The job is to identify the segment where fit is already strong, serve it to the point of devotion, and treat expansion as a separate fit problem rather than assuming it comes free. Balfour's market-definition discipline is how you pick that beachhead deliberately rather than discovering it by accident.

Four fits: when PMF isn't enough

The sharpest dissent in the canon is Brian Balfour's, and it targets the word only. His claim: PMF is necessary but not sufficient. "There are plenty of companies that have all the product market fit signals... but still struggle to grow." His example is Soldsie — strong PMF indicators, never reached venture scale — offered as proof that "product market fit is not the only thing that matters."

His Four Fits framework names the missing pieces: Market-Product, Product-Channel, Channel-Model, and Model-Market fit. "You need all four Fits to grow to $100M+," and critically, "you can't think about the four Fits in isolation because together they form an ecosystem for growth." The fits constrain each other — change your market and your viable channels shift; change your channels and your required economics shift.

One law from the framework is especially load-bearing for PMs who assume distribution is downstream of product: "Products are built to fit with channels. Channels do not mold to products." You cannot bolt a viral product onto an enterprise sales motion, or vice versa; the channel's constraints reach back into what the product must be. Channel-Model fit, which Balfour calls "the ARPU-CAC danger zone," is where many PMF-positive companies quietly die — the product works, but the economics of reaching customers never close. The reconciliation with Andreessen: he was describing the first and most important fit; Balfour is pointing out it is one of four, and that "the only thing that matters" stops being true the moment you have it.

How to get there

The canon does not offer a recipe — by most accounts there isn't one — but the camps agree on a posture. Start from the market and the problem, per Balfour: "Start with the market (problem), then the product (solution)," and go deep on motivations, not just category and audience. Find users with urgent need, per Graham, and serve that narrow group to devotion before widening. Then run the loop deliberately rather than waiting for an epiphany.

Vohra's engine is the most explicit loop on offer: survey, segment, split the roadmap, repeat, with the "very disappointed" score as the metric you are paid to move. Bessemer frames the same iterative spirit with a horticultural metaphor that pushes against the summit myth — PMF is "more like a garden you tend daily" than a peak you climb once. The throughline is that fit is built by tightening the feedback loop between a defined market and a probing product, not by a single heroic insight.

Two practical guards keep the loop honest. First, instrument before you scale — Bessemer's "Time to Value" and "second-bite usage rate" exist to catch a product that demos well but does not pull users back, the failure that paid acquisition can mask for quarters. Second, separate durable revenue from experimental revenue, because nothing delays a team's reckoning with weak fit like a quarter of pilot money that reads as traction. The discipline is less about a method than about refusing to let flattering signals substitute for behavioral ones.

Keeping it — and losing it

The spectrum framing has a consequence teams underweight: fit is not permanent. GoPractice makes the point with the clearest cautionary tale in the canon: PMF strength "can change over time," and as iOS and Android improved, "the product/market fit of BlackBerry got weaker and weaker." Nothing about BlackBerry's product degraded; the market's alternatives improved, and fit is measured relative to those alternatives. You can lose PMF by standing still.

This is why the maintenance camp insists fit is ongoing work. Bessemer's "garden you tend daily" is the operative image; CRV names the warning light — "persistent high churn despite product improvements" — which is precisely the signature of fit eroding while the team optimizes the wrong things. GoPractice's share-of-choice lens is the diagnostic: if users are increasingly choosing alternatives in situations they used to choose you, your fit is decaying regardless of your absolute usage numbers.

The strategic implication for a senior PM is to monitor the lagging signals as vital signs, not just milestones. Retention plateau height, net revenue retention, and organic-versus-paid mix are not one-time PMF certifications; they are the dashboard for a property that drifts. The companies that treat "we have PMF" as a settled fact are the ones GoPractice's BlackBerry example is warning about — fit confirmed once and then assumed, while the market quietly re-fit itself to someone else.

Where this connects — adjacent topics

  • Cohort & retention analytics — the mechanics behind the retention-curve test: how to build cohort triangles, read plateaus, and distinguish the "smile" from a slow decay.
  • Jobs to be Done — Balfour's "motivations behind the problem" is essentially the JTBD lens; the deep version of market-first discovery.
  • Growth loops & the four fits — once PMF is in hand, Balfour's product-channel and channel-model fits are the next constraint; growth-loop design lives here.
  • Unit economics (LTV, CAC, NRR) — channel-model fit is an economics problem; the "ARPU-CAC danger zone" is where PMF-positive companies fail.
  • Customer discovery & user interviews — the qualitative engine feeding the Sean Ellis survey and the High-Expectation Customer profile.
  • North-star metrics — Vohra's "very disappointed" score is one example of choosing the single number a team is paid to move.

References

  1. pmarchive.com ·cited· (T1)

    Marc Andreessen's June 2007 essay "The Only Thing That Matters" defines product/market fit as "being in a good market with a product that can satisfy that market," and argues "the only thing that matters is getting to product/market fit." He quotes Andy Rachleff's law — "When a great team meets a lousy market, market wins. When a lousy team meets a great market, market wins." You can feel fit's presence ("The customers are buying the product just as fast as you can make it—or usage is growing just as fast as you can add more servers") and its absence ("word of mouth isn't spreading, usage isn't growing that fast").

  2. review.firstround.com ·cited· (T1)

    Rahul Vohra (Superhuman founder/CEO) turns PMF into a repeatable engine built on Sean Ellis's survey question, "How would you feel if you could no longer use Superhuman?" The four steps: segment to the "very disappointed" users and build a High-Expectation Customer profile; analyze the "somewhat disappointed" whose main benefit matches the core strength (ignore the "not disappointed"); split the roadmap ~50/50 between doubling down on what users love and removing adoption blockers; survey continuously with the "very disappointed" % as the north-star metric. Superhuman's score rose from 22% to 58% in three quarters.

  3. lennysnewsletter.com ·cited· (T1)

    Lenny Rachitsky curates the canonical PMF signals. Quantitatively, a cohort retention curve that flattens (rather than declining to zero) is the most reliable measure — "If it flattens off at some point, you have probably found product/market fit for some market or audience" (Balfour) and a flattening curve means "there is a group of customers that are finding value in your product, which means you have PMF" (Casey Winters). Qualitatively, Emmett Shear's contrast: "Pushing a boulder: don't have product/market fit. Chasing a boulder: have product/market fit."

  4. a16z.com ·cited· (T1)

    Peter Lauten and David Ulevitch (a16z, 2019) argue product-user fit precedes product-market fit: "The honest version of 'we've got product-market fit on a small cohort of very early power users' is 'we've got product-user fit.'" Product-user fit is "the extent to which you've built the right product for the right user," and is "almost always the thing that matters right before the only thing that ultimately matters" — focus on power-user retention and engagement before scaling.

  5. brianbalfour.com ·cited· (T1)

    Brian Balfour's Four Fits framework: Market-Product, Product-Channel, Channel-Model, and Model-Market fit. "You need all four Fits to grow to $100M+" and "you can't think about the four Fits in isolation because together they form an ecosystem for growth." A key law: "Products are built to fit with channels. Channels do not mold to products." Channel-Model fit is "the ARPU-CAC danger zone."

  6. robwalling.com ·cited· (T1)

    Rob Walling rejects the binary view — "They treat it like a light switch. Either you have it or you don't." Instead, "Product-market fit is a spectrum. You move through stages, and each stage requires different focus areas." His five stages: Pre-PMF, Weak PMF, Emerging PMF, Strong PMF, Mature PMF. The better question is "what stage am I in, and what should I focus on to strengthen it?"

  7. bvp.com ·cited· (T1)

    Bessemer Venture Partners: "PMF isn't binary; it's a spectrum" with light / moderate / strong signal levels (strong = "customers are pulling the product faster than you can deliver"). PMF is "more like a garden you tend daily" than a summit climbed once. Recommended signals: retention by use case, Time to Value, second-bite usage rate, the Sean Ellis 40% threshold, and separating durable revenue (recurring/contractual) from experimental revenue (pilots, novelty spikes).

  8. brianbalfour.com ·cited· (T1)

    Brian Balfour: "There are plenty of companies that have all the product market fit signals... but still struggle to grow." Reaching $100M+ requires four interlocking fits, not one — "Each of these fits influence each other, so you can't think about them in isolation." His example: Soldsie had strong PMF indicators yet failed to reach venture scale, proving "product market fit is not the only thing that matters."

  9. brianbalfour.com ·cited· (T1)

    Brian Balfour reframes "Product Market Fit" as "Market Product Fit": "Start with the market (problem), then the product (solution). Not the other way around." The mistake is "a solution looking for a problem." Define four things first — Category, Who, Problems, Motivations — and "defining the problems and the motivations behind those problems is far more important" than category or audience.

  10. paulgraham.com ·cited· (T1)

    Paul Graham's "How to Get Startup Ideas" (2012): "When a startup launches, there have to be at least some users who really need what they're making — not just people who could see themselves using it one day, but who want it urgently." Founders should solve real problems they understand and validate that urgent demand exists before building, rather than inventing ideas.

  11. lennysnewsletter.com ·cited· (T1)

    Lenny Rachitsky on reading retention curves: "if it flattens, or stops decreasing over time, that means there is a group of users who continue to find value in your product. This is important because it happens to be the best measure of product-market fit." A curve that flattens near ~10% ("only 10% of your users stick around long-term") is "generally a bad retention rate" — the height of the plateau, not just its existence, signals fit strength.

  12. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    Defines PMF as "the degree to which a product satisfies a strong market demand," credits Don Valentine + Andy Rachleff (who first named it) and Marc Andreessen (who popularized it), and stresses that PMF "is not binary—it exists on a spectrum," with early startups needing a high degree of fit. Lists the Sean Ellis 40% rule and five analytics signals (bounce rate, time on site, pages/visit, returning visitors, CLV).

  13. learningloop.io ·cited· (T2)

    The Sean Ellis survey asks "How would you feel if you could no longer use [this product]?" with options Very disappointed / Somewhat disappointed / Not disappointed / N/A. The rule: "≥40% 'very disappointed' = product-market fit, go for growth; <40% = not there yet." Ellis found that "if over 40% of users responded that they would be 'Very disappointed' to stop using the product, there's a great chance that the solution had found its Product-Market fit." Caveat: "If you have to ask whether you have Product/Market Fit, the answer is simple: you don't."

  14. gopractice.io ·cited· (T2)

    GoPractice: "product/market fit is a spectrum. It can range between not being present to being weak, mild, or strong," and "its strength can change over time." Strength = the proportion of situations in which users choose your product over alternatives (100% = maximum fit). PMF can be lost: as iOS/Android improved, "the product/market fit of BlackBerry got weaker and weaker."

  15. crv.com ·cited· (T2)

    CRV (2026): PMF is "the point where a real market wants what you're building and your product reliably satisfies that demand," best treated "as a spectrum rather than a binary event" across Early / Developing / Strong / Extreme levels. Signals: the Sean Ellis 40% test, organic vs. paid growth ("Organic growth is harder to fake"), and "Retention is the most honest signal of product-market fit." Red flag: "Persistent high churn despite product improvements."

  16. learningloop.io ·cited· (T2)

    The Sean Ellis Score = (users answering 'Very disappointed' / total respondents) × 100; the goal is ≥40%. Sean Ellis is "known for coining the term 'growth hacking.'" Caveat: "while the Sean Ellis Test is a useful tool, it is just one part of understanding your users" and "should be used in conjunction with other strategies such as customer feedback, market research, and iterative product development."

  17. opexengine.com ·cited· (T2)

    A roundup of quantitative PMF indicators: Dan Olsen — "Retention rate is the best measure of product-market fit, because it is a behavioral measure of repeat usage"; Andrew Chen — "less than 2% monthly churn," a path to $100k MRR, 5% free-to-paid conversion; Net Dollar Retention above 100% with logo retention above 80% for the expansion stage; and the retention curve should "flatten out to a non-zero retention rate percentage." Organic / word-of-mouth demand is emphasized over paid acquisition.

pmos Source: docs/pmos/primer/2026-06-14_product-market-fit.html · Created using pmos‑learnkit
